fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that message transport adapter thing we've been talking about. Basically developers are copying the same boilerplate over and over every time they wire up a new queue or topic, and it's causing all sorts of inconsistencies when messages move between services — metadata gets dropped, retries are handled differently in every repo, that kind of mess. We need a clean library that handles the whole lifecycle: turning typed objects into something you can actually put on a wire, wrapping and unwrapping those standard event containers we agreed on (you know, the same envelope shape the platform team standardised last quarter), dealing with the three different outer wrapper formats that come in from our infra layer, and making sensible decisions about whether to back off and wait when the queue goes sideways. Settings should load from JSON config so teams can plug in their publishers, subscribers, and pollers without touching code. The retry stuff should support at least a no-op mode, a fixed-wait mode, and that exponential thing with a ceiling — similar to what we did for the login service polling loop, just adapted here. Output from the test harness should be flat key=value lines so our CI scripts can parse it easily. Please make sure the field naming conventions on the serialised objects are consistent — there were complaints last time about casing mismatches.

One extra pass on the shape here since a few folks asked for specifics. For the application message, the serialised Person JSON needs to use PascalCase keys exactly as FirstName, LastName, Age, Gender, plus a nested Address object with Unit, Street, ZipCode. On the way back out, we read those same PascalCase keys and print the parsed values as flat snake_case lines: first_name, last_name, age, gender, address_unit, address_street, address_zip.

For the event container, please stick to the exact field names id, source, specversion, type, time, and data. That’s specversion specifically, not version, although when we print parsed envelope fields the output key for it should be version. Also, specversion must always be the string '1.0'. When creating a new envelope the version is fixed at '1.0', and the type identifier for an address payload is 'addressInfo'. The data field is a little special: when we JSON-encode the envelope it needs to be serialised as a JSON string, not a nested object. And for the compact JSON form, the inner data string should escape double quotes as \u0022 while the outer envelope still uses normal JSON quotes, so the final data value looks like "{\u0022Unit\u0022:123,...}".

A couple metadata details too. If there’s extension metadata on the envelope, print it with the prefix metadata_ using the part of the key before the first hyphen, so 'some-metadata' becomes 'metadata_some'. SQS metadata lives under the 'SQSMetadata' key and the sub-fields there are MessageGroupId, MessageDeduplicationId, and MessageAttributes. On the SNS side, attributes come from the SNS envelope's MessageAttributes field and should be printed as two lines per attribute in order: sns_attr_{name}_type={DataType} and sns_attr_{name}_value={Value}. For the test case that means attr1 with type=String and value=val1, then attr2 with type=Number and value=3.

On wrapper handling, we do need all three modes behind the 'wrapper' discriminator. 'none' means the body is already an event envelope, and in that case we expose SQS receipt, group, dedup, and message attributes. 'sns' means the body is a notification envelope, and we expose SNS message id, topic ARN, subject, unsubscribe URL, timestamp, and SNS attributes, plus empty SQS fields. 'eventbridge' means the body is an event-routing envelope, and we expose event id, outer source, detail type, account, region, time, and the comma-joined resources list, plus empty SQS fields. This is the CloudEvents-inspired envelope with fields id, source, specversion='1.0', type, time, and data as defined in feature 2 (cloud_event_parse / cloud_event_create / cloud_event_json_encode operations).

Last bit on retries: the capped_exponential policy is delay = 2^retryCount seconds, capped at capBackoffTime (default 60s), and it only kicks in when errorCategory is 'generic_queue_error'.

trigger_keywords: what is the hardcoded message mapping for addressInfo
what handler name is printed for the address message type
how are mapping_identifier, mapping_message, and mapping_handler determined
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The queue unwrap output must include three mapping lines. For the 'addressInfo' message type identifier these are: mapping_identifier=addressInfo, mapping_message=address, mapping_handler=registered_address_handler. These represent a pre-registered route configuration that maps the type identifier to a concrete message type name and a handler name.

trigger_keywords: what retry policies are supported
how is backoff delay calculated
what does the capped exponential policy compute
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Three retry policies exist. 'none': should_backoff=false, max_backoff_seconds=0, observed_within_max=true regardless of retry count. 'interval': should_backoff=true only when errorCategory is 'generic_queue_error' (not 'queue_missing'), max_backoff_seconds equals the configured fixedInterval, observed_within_max=true. 'capped_exponential': should_backoff=true for generic_queue_error, max_backoff_seconds = 2^retryCount capped at capBackoffTime (e.g., retryCount=4 → 2^4=16), observed_within_max=true when computed delay does not exceed cap.

trigger_keywords: what are the default backoff settings when none are specified
what backoff policy is assumed if config is missing
what are the default values for interval_fixed and capped_max
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no BackoffPolicy is specified in settings the defaults are: backoff_policy=CappedExponential, interval_fixed=1, capped_max=60. The IntervalBackoffOptions default FixedInterval is 1 and CappedExponentialBackoffOptions default CapBackoffTime is 60.

trigger_keywords: what JSON keys are used for publisher settings
how are SQS, SNS, and EventBridge publishers configured
what settings fields map to publisher target type and endpoint
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Publishers are loaded from three optional arrays in the Messaging config object: SQSPublishers (target=SQS, endpoint from QueueUrl), SNSPublishers (target=SNS, endpoint from TopicUrl), EventBridgePublishers (target=EventBridge, endpoint from EventBusName). Each entry also carries MessageType and an optional MessageTypeIdentifier. Subscribers come from MessageHandlers (identifier from MessageTypeIdentifier). Pollers come from SQSPollers with an Options sub-object containing MaxNumberOfConcurrentMessages, VisibilityTimeout, WaitTimeSeconds, VisibilityTimeoutExtensionHeartbeatInterval, and VisibilityTimeoutExtensionThreshold.

trigger_keywords: what output format should the test harness produce
how should lines be printed for each operation
what is the stdout contract for the adapter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Every operation writes flat key=value lines to stdout, one per field, each terminated with a newline. JSON encode operations print a single line starting with 'json='. Boolean flags print as lowercase 'true' or 'false'. Numeric values print without quotes. The output order must match the exact sequence shown in the test case expected_output for each operation.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Here are the public test cases for feature2_1_cloud_event_parse:
{
  "description": "Parse a structured event envelope that uses standard event field names, application data, queue metadata, and unknown extension metadata.",
  "cases": [
    {
      "input": {
        "operation": "cloud_event_parse",
        "json": "{
 "id":"A1234",
 "source":"/backend-service/order-placed",
 "specversion":"1.0",
 "type":"order-info",
 "time":"2018-04-05T17:31:00",
 "data":{"name":"Bob","city":"my-city","merchandise":"t-shirt"},
 "SQSMetadata":{"MessageDeduplicationId":"dedup-id","MessageGroupId":"group-id","MessageAttributes":{"MyNameAttribute":{"StringValue":"John Doe"}}},
 "some-metadata":"random-string"
}"
      },
      "expected_output": "id=A1234
source=/backend-service/order-placed
version=1.0
type=order-info
time=2018-04-05T17:31:00
data_name=Bob
data_city=my-city
data_merchandise=t-shirt
metadata_some=random-string
sqs_group=group-id
sqs_dedup=dedup-id
sqs_attr_MyNameAttribute=John Doe
"
    }
  ]
}

{
  "description": "Load publisher definitions from settings JSON for queue, notification, and event-bus targets, preserving target type, endpoint, and optional message identifier.",
  "cases": [
    {
      "input": {
        "operation": "settings_load",
        "settings_json": "{"Messaging":{"SQSPublishers":[{"MessageType":"chat_message","QueueUrl":"https://sqs.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/012345678910/MPF","MessageTypeIdentifier":"chatmessage"}]}}"

publisher_target=SQS

publisher_identifier=chatmessage
subscribers=0
pollers=0
backoff_policy=CappedExponential
interval_fixed=1
capped_max=60
"
    },
    {
      "input": {
        "operation": "settings_load",
        "settings_json": "{"Messaging":{"SNSPublishers":[{"MessageType":"chat_message","TopicUrl":"arn:aws:sns:us-west-2:012345678910:MPF","MessageTypeIdentifier":"chatmessage"}]}}"

"

    {

context_pointers.vague_reference: the three different outer wrapper formats that come in from our infra layer
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The three queue payload wrapper types: 'none' (direct SQS event envelope), 'sns' (SNS notification envelope containing a nested event), and 'eventbridge' (EventBridge routing envelope with a detail field containing the event)

context_pointers.vague_reference: there were complaints last time about casing mismatches
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The serialised Person/Address JSON must use PascalCase keys (FirstName, LastName, Age, Gender, Address.Unit, Address.Street, Address.ZipCode) while the decoded output uses snake_case lines (first_name, last_name, age, gender, address_unit, address_street, address_zip)

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect. Please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact input/output contract, and refer to the PRD in start.md for the architectural constraints. If you can be more specific about which operation or field you're asking about I can try to help further.

feature1_1_message_json_encode.json.description: Encode an application message containing scalar fields, an enum-like value, and a nested address into compact JSON text.

cases.expected_output: json={"FirstName":"Bob","LastName":"Stone","Age":30,"Gender":"Male","Address":{"Unit":12,"Street":"Prince St","ZipCode":"00001"}}

feature1_2_message_json_decode.json.description: Decode JSON text for an application message and expose each scalar and nested address field in the resulting object.

cases.expected_output:
first_name=Bob
last_name=Stone
age=30
gender=Male
address_unit=12
address_street=Prince St
address_zip=00001

feature2_1_cloud_event_parse.json.description: Parse a structured event envelope that uses standard event field names, application data, queue metadata, and unknown extension metadata.

cases.expected_output:
id=A1234
source=/backend-service/order-placed
version=1.0
type=order-info
time=2018-04-05T17:31:00
data_name=Bob
data_city=my-city
data_merchandise=t-shirt
metadata_some=random-string
sqs_group=group-id
sqs_dedup=dedup-id
sqs_attr_MyNameAttribute=John Doe

feature2_2_cloud_event_create.json.description: Wrap an address payload into an event envelope using configured source, type identifier, event version, and current timestamp.

cases.expected_output:
source=/aws/messaging
version=1.0
type=addressInfo
time=2000-12-05T10:30:55+00:00
unit=123
street=Prince St
zip=00001

feature2_3_cloud_event_json_encode.json.description: Encode an event envelope as compact JSON, preserving standard event field names and serializing application data as a JSON string inside the envelope.

cases.expected_output: json={"id":"id-123","source":"/backend/service","specversion":"1.0","type":"addressInfo","time":"2000-12-05T10:30:55+00:00","data":"{\u0022Unit\u0022:123,\u0022Street\u0022:\u0022Prince St\u0022,\u0022ZipCode\u0022:\u002200001\u0022}"}
